# ADHD-Friendly Design Rules for Handwritten GoodNotes

## Executive Function Support (ADHD-Critical)

### Time Clarity ⏱️
- **Every page shows read time**: ⏱️ ~3 min (reduces "is this worth my attention?" friction)
- **Locked-in schedule**: Days 1–3–7–14–30 are explicit (eliminates "when should I study?" paralysis)
- **Clear time boundaries**: Page structure enforces 3–5 min chunks (prevents hyperfocus derailment)

### Difficulty Transparency ⚡
- **Every page has a difficulty flag**: Easy / Medium / Hard
- **Prevents overwhelm**: Self-assess BEFORE starting (executive scaffolding)
- **Allows strategic planning**: "I'll do all Easy pages first" (executive function support)

### Progress & Dopamine Feedback ✓
- **Checkboxes after every section**: [ ] → [✓] (small wins = dopamine hits)
- **Visible progress tracking**: "3/3 ✓ You crushed this section!" 
- **Difficulty confidence**: "I picked Easy, and I got it!" = competence boost
- **Streak tracking invitation**: Track your Days 1–3–7–14–30 reviews visually

### Action First (Executive Scaffolding)
- **"Try This Right Now" BEFORE theory**: Concrete action before abstract explanation
- **≤30 seconds to complete**: Lowers activation energy to get started
- **Why it matters is stated**: Explains why the action matters (reduces "why am I doing this?" friction)

---

## Content for Working Memory Limits

### Micro-Chunking Strict Rules
- **ONE page = 3–5 minutes of reading MAXIMUM**
- **One concept per page**: Never combine multiple ideas
- **One idea per bullet**: Never mix two concepts in a single line
- **Max 10 words per bullet**: Reduces cognitive load per eye fixation

### Simple Vocabulary
- **Explain all jargon**: No assumed knowledge
- **Active voice ONLY**: Never passive ("it was discovered") — use active ("scientists discovered")
- **Concrete nouns, action verbs**: "Use memory palace" not "implement mnemonic strategies"
- **8th grade reading level**: Simple, direct, conversational

### Real-World Examples FIRST
- **Application before theory**: Show the use case BEFORE explaining why it works
- **Specific scenarios**: Not generic "you can use this to..." but "forgot your shopping list? Try this..."
- **Personal relevance**: "something YOU care about" — not abstract

### Flashcard Forced Retrieval
- **Q&A format**: Forces brain to GENERATE answers (not just recognize)
- **Minimal text to read**: Reduces working memory load during active recall
- **Common traps**: Include "what NOT to do" (prevents errors)

---

## Visuals for ADHD Brains

### Not Decorative — Every Visual Communicates
- **No fluff**: Every diagram, table, or icon serves a purpose
- **Simple ASCII diagrams**: Flows, decision trees, relationships (not complex graphics)
- **Tables over paragraphs**: Side-by-side comparison reduces working memory load
- **One visual per page minimum**: Breaks up text, helps visual learners, provides scanning reference

### Visual Examples for ADHD

**Simple Flow:**
```
Start → [Do This] → [Check Result] → Success/Adjust
```

**Comparison Table** (easier than prose):
```
| When | Use This | Why | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Grocery store | Memory palace | Brain loves places | 30 sec |
| Before meeting | Quick facts | Forces recall | 2 min |
| Study time | Spaced rep | Long-term memory | 1-2 min |
```

**Decision Tree** (scaffolds executive function):
```
Do I have < 5 minutes?
  → YES: Try the "Right Now" action
  → NO: Read full explanation tomorrow
```

### Icon & Emoji for Quick Scanning
- **⏱️ Time**: How long is this section?
- **📚 Section**: What chapter/topic is this?
- **⚡ Difficulty**: Is this easy or hard?
- **🎯 Action**: What do I do right now?
- **✓ Checkpoint**: Did I learn this?
- **🗓️ Dates**: When do I review this?

---

## Handwritten Aesthetic

### Warm, Friendly Tone
- **Conversational language**: "You'll learn..." not "The student will acquire knowledge of..."
- **Use contractions**: "you're", "don't", "can't" (feels personal)
- **Direct address**: "YOU" not "the reader"
- **Encouraging**: "3/3 ✓ You crushed this section!"

### Typography for Readability
- **Generous line spacing**: Double-spaced or more (not dense)
- **Clear heading hierarchy**: ### (section) > #### (subsection) > regular text
- **Short line length**: Prevent eye strain during scanning
- **Bullet points**: Not dense paragraphs

### Squared Paper Background (Tactile)
- **Graph paper grid**: 7mm horizontal + vertical lines
- **Organized feel**: Helps with visual anchoring
- **Handwritten aesthetic**: Feels like a personal notebook in GoodNotes
- **Tactile engagement**: Kinesthetic learners benefit from grid structure

---

## Section Structure for ADHD

Every page should have these sections (in this order):

1. **Header** (⏱️ ~3 min | 📚 Chapter X | ⚡ Easy/Medium/Hard)
2. **What You'll Learn** (1 sentence hook explaining personal benefit)
3. **The Core Idea** (60-second ultra-simple explanation)
4. **Visual Summary** (diagram, table, or flowchart)
5. **Real-World Application** (concrete scenarios, not theory)
6. **Quick Facts** (Q&A flashcard format)
7. **Try This Right Now** (≤30 sec action + why it matters)
8. **Remember This** (spaced rep schedule: Days 1, 3, 7, 14, 30)
9. **Checkpoint** (✓ boxes for dopamine feedback + progress note)

---

## What ADHD-Friendly Looks Like

### ✓ Good ADHD Section
```
⏱️ ~4 min read  |  📚 Chapter 3: Memory  |  ⚡ Difficulty: Easy

## What You'll Learn
Forget your shopping list? Remember it without your phone — using your brain's natural talent for remembering places.

## The Core Idea (60 seconds)
The "method of loci" (memory palace) tricks your brain into storing items. You mentally place each item in a familiar location (your home, street, gym). Your brain LOVES remembering places, so this works. Done.

## Visual Summary
Your home's journey:
```
Front Door → Living Room → Kitchen → Bedroom
[Keys here]  [Wallet here] [Phone] [Shoes]
```

## Real-World Application
- Grocery list: No app needed (works while driving!)
- Meeting notes: Remember action items on the drive home
- Presentations: Recall talking points before standing up

## Quick Facts
Q: Why does this work?  
A: Your brain stores locations better than abstract lists.

Q: How many items can you remember?  
A: Usually 5–10 before it gets overwhelming.

Q: What's the trick?  
A: Vivid, bizarre mental images stick better than boring ones.

## Try This Right Now (30 seconds)
1. Think of your home's front door.
2. Mentally place your car keys on the doormat.
3. Mentally place your wallet on the door handle.
4. Close your eyes; mentally walk to these spots.
→ Result: You remembered 2 items without writing!

## Remember This
🗓️ Day 1: Memory palace = placing items in familiar locations
🗓️ Day 3: Your brain loves PLACES, not lists
🗓️ Day 7: Try this for grocery shopping, meetings, presentations
🗓️ Day 14: Bizarre images work BETTER than boring ones
🗓️ Day 30: Combine with spaced rep for lists > 10 items

## Checkpoint ✓
- [ ] I can place 2 items in my mental home (without looking back)
- [ ] I know ONE place I'll use this
- [ ] I did the 30-second try-it

**Progress: 3/3 ✓ Memory palace unlocked!**
```

### ✗ Avoid This (Not ADHD-Friendly)
```
Spatial memory techniques leverage the brain's intrinsic predisposition toward 
environmental encoding, utilizing mnemonic strategies historically known as the 
method of loci. Research indicates that students who employ such encoding mechanisms 
demonstrate enhanced retention rates...
```
(Too dense, too academic, no action, no time estimate, no difficulty flag, no personal benefit)

---

## ADHD-Friendly Checklist (Before Publishing)

- [ ] **Time labeled**: ⏱️ visible at top (read time estimate)
- [ ] **Difficulty rated**: ⚡ Easy/Medium/Hard visible at top
- [ ] **Hook is personal**: "What YOU'll Learn" = personal benefit, not topic name
- [ ] **Core Idea ≤ 60 sec**: Ultra-simple, 3–4 sentences max, includes 1 example
- [ ] **Visual first, text second**: Diagram/table BEFORE paragraphs
- [ ] **Application before theory**: "You can use this to..." comes BEFORE explanation
- [ ] **Flashcards force recall**: Q&A format (not just facts to memorize)
- [ ] **Try This is ≤30 sec**: Concrete action + observable result
- [ ] **Spaced rep is EXPLICIT**: Days 1, 3, 7, 14, 30 with specific facts
- [ ] **Checkboxes present**: [ ] boxes for dopamine feedback
- [ ] **Progress message**: "3/3 ✓ You crushed this!" after checkpoints
- [ ] **Whitespace generous**: Room to breathe between sections
- [ ] **Hyperlinks included**: Can jump non-linearly (scattered attention support)
- [ ] **No jargon without explanation**: All terms defined
- [ ] **One idea per bullet**: Never combine concepts
- [ ] **Max 10 words per bullet**: Reduces cognitive load
- [ ] **Active voice only**: Never passive ("was learned" → "you learned")
- [ ] **Icon/emoji used**: ⏱️, 📚, ⚡, ✓, 🗓️ for quick scanning
